category_title: Students with academic anxiety who use physical gestures to conceptualize mathematical terms
minimal_context: Luke is working on a math problem with a negative exponent in the denominator during a small group tutoring session.
dialogue:
TUTOR: Let's look at this next problem on the paper. It has a negative exponent in the denominator. Go ahead and try to solve it.

STUDENT: I worked on this but I can't figure out how to solve it.

TUTOR: What do you think you should do next?

STUDENT: When you switch right? (Speaks in an unsure, questioning way)

TUTOR: Can you explain what you mean?

STUDENT: Uh…The same thing, but twist, right? (TWISTING his hand back and forth in a circular motion with his palm facing toward Matt)

options.A: Point to specific mathematical symbols (the negative exponent in the denominator) and ask a guiding question about a prerequisite term: 'If you wanted to get rid of this and make it one number you have to multiply by the reciprocal. Do you remember what that is?'
options.B: Provide high rates of adult attention directed at appropriate behavior, using a timer on a fixed 1-min interval schedule to give specific performance feedback (e.g., 'great job working,' 'keep trying').
options.C: Allow the student to work at his own pace on the math problem and take a break from the small group tutoring session as and when he wants.
options.D: Terminate demands and remove the math paper for 10 seconds contingent upon the occurrence of the behavior during the small group tutoring session.
